behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: The failure is a logic bug in the grouping function (e.g., using wrong field, incorrect equality check). This is catchable at Unit level by providing mock entries with diverse trace IDs from simulated sources. However, real systems with concurrent ingestion or I/O timing might reveal gaps that Unit tests miss, hence Integration probability.
behaviors.test_description: Create 20 log entries with 5 unique trace IDs, each appearing 4 times (simulating 4 different microservices). Call the grouping function and verify: (1) exactly 5 groups created, (2) each group has 4 entries, (3) entries from different services in same group are all present. This directly tests whether the grouping logic associates by trace ID correctly.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: The core failure is whether the ordering and skew-adjustment logic work correctly. This is testable at Unit level by providing entries with specific timestamps (including 500ms skew). However, in real systems, determining the 'baseline' time and handling actual timestamp formats from multiple sources might require Integration or real network timing.
behaviors.test_description: Create 10 log entries with timestamps spread across 500ms (e.g., T, T+100ms, T+300ms from different services). Call the timeline ordering function and verify: (1) entries are ordered correctly with skew adjustment applied, (2) an entry with T+600ms (beyond 500ms tolerance) is not incorrectly 'fixed', (3) the final timeline is in ascending order by adjusted timestamp. This catches ordering and boundary bugs.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in the percentile calculation or comparison logic (e.g., using median instead of p99, wrong threshold). This is testable at Unit level by providing request latencies and verifying p99 is calculated and compared correctly. Real systems with varying baselines might need Integration, but the core anomaly detection is a pure logic operation.
behaviors.test_description: Create two latency datasets: (1) baseline with 100 requests from 100–200ms (p99≈200ms), (2) spike with 100 requests from 100–800ms (p99≈600ms). Call spike detection and verify: (1) baseline returns no spike (600 is not > 3×200), (2) spike is flagged, (3) edge case—p99 exactly 3× baseline is not flagged (must be strictly greater). This tests percentile calculation and threshold logic.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: While JSON parsing logic alone could be tested at Unit level (mocking json.loads), the realistic failure mode requires I/O: a stream arrives with valid and malformed entries mixed, parsing fails mid-stream, and the pipeline must skip and count correctly. Stream buffering, concurrent counter updates, and position-tracking bugs are invisible to Unit mocks. Integration testing with real file I/O (or System with network) catches these.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test file with 100 valid JSON log entries and 5 malformed ones (missing braces, invalid escapes, truncated) interspersed. Run the ingestion pipeline on the file and verify: (1) all 100 valid entries are processed, (2) pipeline does not crash, (3) malformed counter increments exactly to 5, (4) if concurrent, counter is atomic (no races), (5) error log contains details of skipped entries. This tests real stream error handling.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: Throughput and capacity are inherently system-level concerns. Real failures come from: (1) queue/buffer saturation under load, (2) memory exhaustion, (3) GC pauses, (4) I/O bottleneck. None of these can be tested with mocks—you must run real I/O at the target throughput and measure real system behavior. Since logs arrive 'from multiple microservices' (implying network), System (real network I/O) is more realistic than Integration (local files).
behaviors.test_description: Set up a high-volume log source producing 10,000 entries/second (either via simulated network stream or high-speed file replay) and run the ingestion pipeline for 10 seconds (100k total entries). Verify: (1) all 100,000 entries are processed (no drops), (2) pipeline does not fall behind (queue/buffer remains bounded), (3) memory usage stays within limits, (4) no loss of trace IDs or ordering fidelity, (5) anomaly detection and report generation complete within acceptable time. This directly measures whether the system can sustain the required throughput.
